content:
PYTHON BASICS
Most Python developers learn config files too late. Here’s everything you need to know — from INI to TOML — with real code you can steal today.

2 min read
Just now

--

You’ve probably been in this situation before.
You have a Python script that runs perfectly on your own computer.
The database host is hardcoded. The API key is right there in main.py. The port number is just a random value on any line of your code.

Then you deploy it to production and everything stops working. Or even worse, you accidentally upload your API key to a public GitHub repository and wake up to a scary email from AWS in the middle of the night.
Configuration files are designed to solve these kinds of problems. They keep your code separate from the way it is set up to run. Still, many Python developers either ignore them or use them incorrectly.
Hardcoding values might seem simple at first, but it actually creates technical debt. This approach feels quick now, but it leads to three real problems later :

• You lose environment portability. The database URL you use for development is not the same as the one for production. If both are in your source code, you end up managing separate branches or messy if-else statements just to deploy.

• Security becomes a real problem. When you put credentials in your code, they can end up in version control. Even if you remove them later, they stay in the git history. GitHub’s secret scanning tool exists for a reason, because this happens all the time.

• Working with others becomes difficult. If five developers all need different local settings, no one should have to change the same Python file just to get the app running on their own computer.
Configuration files create a clear boundary. Your code stays in .py files, your settings go in config files, and your secrets belong in environment variables or a vault. This separation is what professionals use when they release software.
The Four Config Formats Python Developers Should Know
Python supports several configuration formats. Each one has its pros and cons, so the best choice depends on how complex your project is.
